topics: Loṭ Lakāra — giving commands and requests in Sanskrit
subject: Sanskrit

sections.heading: 🛒 Quest Begins: Rakesh Goes to the Market!
sections.body: Imagine your mom sends you to the nearby kirana store with a shopping list — that is exactly what happens here! Rakesh's mother (Amba) tells him that his father will come home late from the office. So she gives Rakesh a list of things to buy and sends him to the market (आपणम्). Just like when your family sends you to buy milk or bread, Rakesh must be brave and do the errand on his own. This is his quest!
keyPoints: Amba gives Rakesh a shopping list called नामावली (nāmāvalī) — a list of items to buy.
Rakesh must go to the market called आपणम् (āpaṇam) and buy everything carefully.
Amba warns him to watch the traffic carefully before crossing the road — just like your parents do!

sections.heading: 🧺 The Shopping Spell: What Did Rakesh Buy?
sections.body: At the shop, Rakesh shows the shopkeeper (āpaṇikaḥ) the list. The list has items like मुद्गः (moong dal), शर्करा (sugar), गुडः (jaggery), विदलम् (pulses/dal), and सर्षपः (mustard seeds). Rakesh also buys a chocolate (चाकलेहम्) and a blue pen (नीलवर्णा लेखनी) for himself! It is just like going to a store and buying groceries plus a little treat for yourself. The total comes to 490 rupees and Rakesh pays 500 rupees, getting 10 rupees back as change.
keyPoints: Rakesh buys grocery items: moong dal, sugar, jaggery, pulses, mustard seeds.
He also buys one chocolate and one blue pen as extras — smart wizard move!
Total cost = 490 rupees. He pays 500 and gets 10 rupees change (अवशिष्टम्).

sections.heading: ⚡ The Power of Loṭ Lakāra — The Command Spell!
sections.body: Now comes the real magical power of this chapter — the Loṭ Lakāra (लोट् लकारः)! In Sanskrit, when you want to tell someone to DO something — like 'read!', 'come!', 'sit!' — you use special verb forms called Loṭ Lakāra. Think of it like a wizard casting a command spell. When your cricket coach says 'Hit the ball!' or your teacher says 'Open your books!' — that is a command. In Sanskrit, every command has a different form depending on whether you are talking about one person, two people, or many people.
keyPoints: Loṭ Lakāra is used to give commands (आज्ञा) or make polite requests (प्रार्थना) — like 'Please come' or 'Sit down!'
Example: पठ (you read!), पठतु (let him read), पठन्तु (let them read).
There are three persons and three numbers — so the verb form changes, just like in other lakāras you have learned before.

sections.heading: 🗣️ Loṭ Lakāra in Real Life — Commands All Around Us!
sections.body: Think about Diwali time — your mom says 'Clean your room!' or during a cricket match your captain says 'Run fast!' — these are all commands! In this chapter, Amba uses Loṭ Lakāra when she tells Rakesh: 'Go to the market! Bring the things! Watch the traffic!' The chapter also has beautiful Sanskrit shlokas (prayers) using Loṭ Lakāra — like wishing that everyone be happy (सुखिनः सन्तु), that rivers flow freely, and that the earth be green. These are prayers using the command form — like telling the universe what to do!
keyPoints: Amba's commands to Rakesh — 'आपणं गच्छ (go to market)', 'वस्तूनि आनय (bring the things)' — are all Loṭ Lakāra.
Prayers like 'सर्वे भवन्तु सुखिनः' (may all be happy) also use Loṭ Lakāra to express wishes.
Tip: If a verb ends in 'तु' (like गच्छतु, पठतु), it is usually a Loṭ Lakāra command for one person!

sections.heading: 🏆 Mastery Unlocked: Be Like Rakesh — A Responsible Young Hero!
sections.body: This chapter teaches us two big things together — how to have a shopping conversation in Sanskrit AND how to use command verbs (Loṭ Lakāra). Rakesh is like a young hero on a quest — he listens to his mother, goes to the market confidently, shops smartly, counts money correctly, and even gets a chocolate as a reward! You can now give commands in Sanskrit, write shopping lists, and understand how Sanskrit prayers use these same verb forms. That is true mastery!
keyPoints: You can now hold a basic shopping conversation in Sanskrit — asking for items, knowing prices, and giving change.
You have mastered Loṭ Lakāra — the command/request verb form in Sanskrit.
Life lesson from the quest: Be helpful at home, be careful on roads, and always say धन्यवादः (thank you)!

summary: In Chapter 12 'त्वम् आपणं गच्छ' (You Go to the Market), young Rakesh goes on a real-life quest — his mother sends him to the market with a shopping list (नामावली) to buy groceries like moong dal, sugar, jaggery, pulses, and mustard seeds. At the shop, he talks to the shopkeeper in Sanskrit, finds out the prices, adds up the total (490 rupees), pays 500, and gets change back. Along the way, he also buys a chocolate and a blue pen for himself! The chapter introduces the powerful Loṭ Lakāra (लोट् लकारः) — the verb form used to give commands and make requests in Sanskrit, just like when a coach says 'Run!' or a teacher says 'Read!' Students learn how this form changes for one, two, or many people, and see it used in everyday commands as well as beautiful Sanskrit prayers wishing happiness and peace for all.
